cuisine: Global / Multi-regional (Middle Eastern, South Asian, Mediterranean, European)
region_of_origin: Iran (Persia); also cultivated in India, Spain, and Greece

ontology.foodex2_description: Saffron — dried stigmas of Crocus sativus, used as a spice and colouring agent

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical culinary serving of saffron is approximately 0.1–0.5 g (one small pinch), used to flavour and colour a dish for 2–4 people.

nutrient_source.title: Saffron, dried — HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 02043)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) and USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol) sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo as provided in the input record (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, sugar, and additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for dried saffron (FDC ID 170926). Values are consistent between sources.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for dried saffron — analogous estimate based on mixed spice/herb category; saffron used in trace culinary quantities (0.1–0.5 g per dish)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate); Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021 (spice category reference)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried saffron exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. Saffron is a high-carbohydrate spice by weight (65.37 g/100g) but is consumed exclusively in trace amounts (0.1–0.5 g per dish), making its glycemic impact negligible in practice. A GI of approximately 45 (low) is estimated by analogy with other dried aromatic spices and herbs in the University of Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving (0.3 g serving): GL = (45 × 0.196) / 100 = 0.09, confirming negligible glycemic impact per culinary use.

hypertension.key_factor: Exceptionally favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio; very low per-serving sodium contribution
hypertension.guidance: Saffron provides only ~0.04 mg sodium and ~5.2 mg potassium per typical 0.3 g culinary serving, making it entirely safe for hypertensive individuals; its high potassium content (1724 mg/100g) supports vascular health, and emerging evidence suggests safranal and crocin may exert mild vasodilatory effects.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible per-serving carbohydrate and glycemic load; bioactive compounds may improve insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: At culinary doses of 0.1–0.5 g per dish, saffron contributes less than 0.33 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of under 0.1 per serving, posing no glycemic risk; crocin and safranal have demonstrated insulin-sensitising and anti-hyperglycaemic effects in peer-reviewed clinical studies, supporting its use in a diabetes-friendly diet.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Antioxidant carotenoids (crocin, crocetin) with demonstrated cardioprotective and anti-atherogenic properties
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saffron's carotenoid compounds crocin and crocetin have been shown in clinical trials to reduce LDL oxidation, lower triglycerides, and inhibit platelet aggregation; at culinary doses it contributes negligible saturated fat (0.005 g per 0.3 g serving) and zero cholesterol, making it a heart-safe spice for cardiovascular patients.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory polyphenols (crocin, crocetin, safranal) with TNF-alpha and IL-1beta inhibitory activity
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saffron's bioactive compounds have demonstrated inhibition of pro-inflammatory cytokines TNF-alpha and IL-1beta relevant to RA pathogenesis; culinary use at 0.1–0.5 g per dish is safe, while high-dose supplementation (>1.5 g/day) should be medically supervised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Saffron (Crocus sativus) is the world's most expensive spice by weight, consisting of the hand-harvested dried stigmas of the saffron crocus flower. Each flower produces only three stigmas, requiring approximately 150,000 flowers to yield 1 kg of dried saffron. The spice presents as fine, deep red-orange threads with a characteristic earthy, floral, and slightly metallic aroma derived from its key volatile compound safranal, and a golden-yellow colour imparted by the carotenoid crocin. Saffron is used in trace culinary quantities — typically 0.1 to 0.5 grams per dish — to flavour and colour rice dishes (biryani, paella, risotto Milanese), stews, soups, breads, and confections. Despite its high per-100g carbohydrate content (65.37 g) and energy (310 kcal), actual per-serving nutrient contribution is negligible due to the tiny quantities used. Nutritionally, saffron is rich in potassium (1724 mg/100g), iron (11.1 mg/100g), vitamin C (80.8 mg/100g), magnesium (264 mg/100g), and B vitamins, though these are only clinically relevant at supplemental doses. Its bioactive carotenoids — crocin, crocetin, and picrocrocin — and the volatile safranal have been studied for antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, neuroprotective, antidepressant, and cardioprotective effects. Saffron is suitable for individuals with hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and cardiovascular disease when used in culinary amounts. It is vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher. Saffron is cultivated primarily in Iran (which produces over 90% of global supply), followed by India (Kashmir), Spain, and Greece.
common_user_queries: How much saffron should I use per dish?
What are the health benefits of saffron?
Is saffron safe for high blood pressure?

colouring agent for breads and pastries
used in Middle Eastern and South Asian desserts

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (confidence 0.95 per input record). Additional micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170926) for dried saffron, which is an exact match. Both sources are approved primary sources under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI is an analogous estimate (low confidence for GI only) but is clearly documented as such.
notes: 1. Sugar_g is set to 0.0: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy reports 0.0 g sugar per 100g for dried saffron (confirmed zero per approved source). Trans_fat_g is set to 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for dried saffron. Vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg are set to 0.0: confirmed absent in plant-derived spice per USDA SR Legacy. 2. Fields set to -1 (data not available in approved sources): vitamin_a_mcg_rae — not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for saffron in RAE units; folate_mcg_dfe — not reported in DFE units in available approved sources; vitamin_e_mg — not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for saffron; selenium_mcg — not reported in HPB or USDA SR Legacy for saffron. 3. GI is an analogous estimate (GI ~45, low) as no direct GI measurement for saffron exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 0.3 g serving is 0.09, confirming negligible glycemic impact at culinary doses. 4. Saffron is used exclusively in trace culinary amounts (0.1–0.5 g per dish); per-100g nutritional values are not representative of real-world intake and should always be contextualised by serving size. 5. Ontology codes: FoodOn URI and SNOMED CT code are defensible matches for saffron as a food ingredient/substance; FoodEx2 code A00LC is used for saffron spice. Reviewers should verify FoodEx2 code against the current EFSA FoodEx2 browser if precision is required.
